title: Analogue Comforts
passage:
"Everyone assured us the format was finished," says Priya Mehta, who opened a board-game café in a former launderette three winters ago. Her tone is dry rather than triumphant. When the first of these places appeared on British high streets, most landlords treated them as a passing ___(1)___, a fad that would fold once the thrill of rolling dice in public wore thin. The prediction looked safe. Rents were rising, screens were everywhere, and an evening spent pushing wooden pieces around a table seemed a strange thing to pay for.

Yet the cafés did not fold. If anything, their ___(2)___ has quietly deepened. Membership schemes now run waiting lists, and publishers court the venues for their launches. What the sceptics had missed was that the appeal was never really about the games. Mehta is blunt about it. "People are not starved of entertainment," she says. "They are starved of an excuse to sit across from someone for two hours with no screen between them." The table, in other words, sells company, and company has proved stubbornly ___(3)___ to the download.

That does not make the model an easy one. Margins are narrow, and a café that misjudges its stock can ___(4)___ its slim margins overnight, its shelves clogged with games nobody wants to play. The owners who last treat curation as seriously as a bookseller treats a front table. They rotate titles, lay on tutors for the more daunting rulebooks, and resist the temptation to chase every fashionable release. Where the earliest operators tried to stock everything, the survivors have learned to choose.

The lesson is not that screens are in retreat; they plainly are not. It is that a certain kind of unhurried, face-to-face pleasure has turned out to be far more ___(5)___ than the analysts assumed. The dice, it seems, were never really the point.

questions.rationale: Suffix-rhymed -ty set. Frame 'treated them as a passing ___, a fad' demands a synonym for a short-lived enthusiasm: 'a passing novelty' is the established collocation and is glossed by the apposition 'a fad'. austerity = collocation_misfit (right shape, no '*passing austerity' collocation, and wrong sense). loyalty and casualty are real -ty nouns that produce no idiomatic reading with 'a passing ___' in this context.

Suffix-rhymed -ity set. 'their ___ has quietly deepened', evidenced by waiting lists and courting publishers, is growth in reach: popularity. notoriety = polarity/sense mirror (negative pole where the positive narrative 'did not fold' demands upside). prosperity = sense/evidence misfit ('prosperity deepened' is unremarkable English, so this is not a collocation misfit; prosperity names the cafes' own wealth, whereas the cited evidence — waiting lists and publishers courting the venues — measures demand and reach, and the next paragraph's 'Margins are narrow' explicitly denies profitability). familiarity = near-synonym/evidence misfit ('familiarity has deepened' is perfectly idiomatic, so this is not a collocation misfit; familiarity would mean the cafes are merely better known, whereas waiting lists and publishers competing to launch there evidence rising demand and desirability — popularity — not bare recognition).

Polarity gap. The whole passage argues the cafes withstood digital substitution ('did not fold'); 'proved stubbornly ___ to the download' = held out against it: resistant ('stubbornly resistant to' is the locking collocation). vulnerable = polarity_mirror (the negative pole a skimmer expecting decline would grab). superior = scope/sense error (the text claims survival, not a quality ranking over downloads). hostile = sense/direction error ('hostile to' is a standard collocation, so this is not a collocation misfit; it fails on sense — 'hostile to the download' would cast company as an active antagonist attacking downloads, whereas the passage has company passively holding out against substitution, i.e. defensive endurance, not aggression).

Collocation + polarity gap. 'misjudges its stock can ___ its slim margins overnight' needs a verb that damages margins: 'erode margins' is the standard collocation and matches the negative cause. inflate = polarity_mirror (a positive outcome where misjudgement demands a loss). dilute = mechanism/sense misfit ('dilute margins' is itself a standard business collocation, so the discriminator is not collocation; dilute names a specific mechanism — pulling an average margin down by mixing in lower-margin business, a compositional effect — whereas the sentence has already-slim margins destroyed suddenly 'overnight' by dead, unsellable stock, which is erosion, i.e. eating them away, not dilution by admixture). squander = collocation_misfit (squander takes money/chances/opportunities, not 'margins').

Suffix-rhymed -able set. 'unhurried, face-to-face pleasure has turned out to be far more ___ than the analysts assumed', closing an argument about lasting appeal: durable. disposable = polarity_mirror (the negative pole; the passage argues the pleasure lasts, not that it is throwaway). negotiable = collocation_misfit ('more negotiable' has no sense here). portable = sense misfit (physical transportability is irrelevant to the claim).

generator_meta.gap_marker_convention: ___(n)___ inline in passage; questions[] carries gap n as q_index n with prompt 'Gap (n)'
generator_meta.schema_note: 5 gaps => 5 questions[]; exceeds candidate-item.schema maxItems:4 and mech M-SCHEMA len<=4. See NOTES.md.
